description: This figure presents a comprehensive microstructural characterization of four Fe–20Mn–9Al–1.5C austenitic low-density steels with varying Ni and Cr additions: 3Cr, 2Ni, 2Ni3Cr, and 2Ni5Cr. The top rows display EBSD inverse pole figure (IPF) maps (a1–d1) and phase maps (a2–d2) revealing equiaxed fully-recrystallized single-phase FCC austenite (γ) with average grain sizes ranging from 3.51 μm to 8.95 μm. The middle rows show TEM bright-field images (e–h) confirming single-phase austenitic structures, and dark-field images (i–l) taken from {110} LRO or {110} κ reflections revealing distinct nanoscale precipitates: 3Cr and 2Ni5Cr steels (high Cr content) contain homogeneously distributed nanosized long-range ordering (LRO) domains (~1.75–2.14 nm), while 2Ni and 2Ni3Cr steels (high Ni content) exhibit orthorhombic κ-carbides with cuboidal morphology, with the 2Ni steel showing the largest average size (~10.69 nm). The bottom row presents selected area diffraction patterns (m–p) recorded along the [011]γ zone axis, showing faint superlattice spots at 1/2{200}γ positions for LRO-containing steels and distinct κ-carbide superlattice reflections for Ni-rich steels, confirming the atomic ordering. These results demonstrate that Cr addition suppresses κ-carbide formation in favor of LRO domains, whereas Ni promotes κ-carbide precipitation, providing critical evidence for the alloying synergy strategy.
